title: The Warfare Of The Future Is Already Here | NOEMA
gist: Drawing on an interview with Eric Schmidt, the piece argues AI-assisted drones are the largest revolution in military affairs in history, inverting battlefield doctrine (robots first, humans last), making cheap precision-mass weapons dominant, rendering exquisite legacy platforms obsolete, and destabilizing nuclear deterrence.

themes.theme: War of systems replaces war of platforms

insights.claim: Eric Schmidt argues the right unit of analysis in modern war is not the drone, missile or launcher but the integrated architecture that lets a military see, decide, communicate, strike, survive and update faster than the adversary.
insights.speaker: NATHAN GARDELS
insights.so_what: Defense investors should favor command-and-control software, networking and integration layers over individual hardware platforms.
insights.quote: The right unit of analysis isn't the drone or the missile or the launcher. It's the integrated architecture that lets a military see, decide, communicate, strike, survive and update faster than your adversary.
insights.locator: From A War Of Platforms To A War Of Systems

insights.claim: Eric Schmidt predicts future battlefield doctrine will invert so that robots are sent in first to absorb fire and clear the battlefield, with humans going in last rather than first as they do today.
insights.speaker: NATHAN GARDELS
insights.so_what: Signals durable demand for attritable/expendable autonomous ground and air systems over manned platforms.
insights.quote: In the next war, that principle inverts. You send the robots in first to absorb fire and clear the battlefield.
insights.locator: Second, every weapon will be supported by AI

insights.claim: Eric Schmidt forecasts a shift from humans 'in the loop' authorizing each shot to humans 'on the loop' of a distributed system, supervising and intervening but not authorizing every individual strike.
insights.speaker: NATHAN GARDELS
insights.so_what: Autonomy and delegated-lethality software will face less human-authorization friction than current policy assumes; watch autonomous-weapons regulation.
insights.quote: The future will entail 'humans on the loop' of a distributed system, rather than always 'in the loop' — supervising, auditing and intervening when something looks wrong, but not necessarily authorizing each individual shot.
insights.locator: The question then moves into what systems replace the human operator

themes.theme: Drone-driven battlefield economics and precision mass

insights.claim: In March 2026, 96% of Russian casualties were caused by Ukrainian drone units, according to Eric Schmidt.
insights.speaker: NATHAN GARDELS
insights.so_what: Quantifies how decisively cheap drones now dominate kinetic effects, reframing where defense dollars should flow.
insights.quote: In March of this year, 96% of Russian casualties were caused by Ukrainian drone units.

insights.claim: The drone operator has become the highest-value target on the battlefield, with Ukrainians prizing the killing of a Russian drone operator more than killing a tank.
insights.speaker: NATHAN GARDELS
insights.so_what: Implies rising value in operator-protection, remote-operation and fully autonomous targeting to remove the human from harm.
insights.quote: The drone operator is now the highest-value target on the battlefield — Ukrainians prize killing a Russian drone operator even more than killing a tank.

insights.claim: Eric Schmidt argues warfare has entered an era of 'precision mass' because cheap drones, cheap GPS and cheap sensors have eliminated the old trade-off between firing inaccurate artillery en masse and firing small numbers of expensive precision weapons.
insights.speaker: NATHAN GARDELS
insights.so_what: Commoditized sensing/guidance components become the key value chain; legacy expensive precision munitions lose their premium.
insights.quote: We are in an era of 'precision mass' in warfare. In conflict, you used to have to choose between mass and accuracy.
insights.locator: We are in an era of 'precision mass'

insights.claim: Eric Schmidt cites a $500 FPV drone going through a single window as cleaner than mass artillery flattening a city block, arguing FPV drones produced minimal collateral damage in engagements he observed in Ukraine.
insights.speaker: NATHAN GARDELS
insights.so_what: Cost-per-effect collapse (~$500/precision strike) is the core economic disruptor for procurement budgets.
insights.quote: The dumb war of mass artillery flattening a city block is far worse than a $500 drone going through a single window.
insights.locator: You can now field huge numbers of weapons

themes.theme: Procurement obsolescence and industrial capacity gap

insights.claim: Russia aims to produce 1,000 Shahed drones a day to fire at Ukraine, while Lockheed Martin produced 600 Patriot interceptors in all of last year, illustrating a mass-production mismatch in modern war.
insights.speaker: NATHAN GARDELS
insights.so_what: The cost and volume asymmetry favors cheap-mass producers; interceptor-makers face an unsustainable economics problem unless costs fall.
insights.quote: The Russians are aiming to produce 1,000 Shahed drones a day to fire against Ukraine, while Lockheed Martin produced 600 Patriot interceptors last year.
insights.locator: The Wrong Kind Of Weapons For A New Kind of War

insights.claim: Eric Schmidt argues a great deal of current US military spending is going to exquisite, expensive platforms whose purpose, training pipelines and budgets rest on assumptions the war in Ukraine has already disproven.
insights.speaker: NATHAN GARDELS
insights.so_what: Bearish signal for legacy prime-contractor flagship programs; bullish for cheap-abundant-systems startups and industrial capacity buildouts.
insights.quote: American military doctrine is still organized around exquisite, expensive platforms designed for a kind of conflict that is no longer the one being fought.

insights.claim: Inexpensive drone swarms launched by Iran inflicted the most damage on the oil-and-gas infrastructure of US-allied Gulf States during the hot war with Iran.
insights.speaker: NATHAN GARDELS
insights.so_what: Critical energy infrastructure is now cheaply attackable; raises insurance, hardening and air-defense spend in the Gulf.
insights.quote: the most damage done to the integral infrastructure of the oil-and-gas-rich U.S.-allied Gulf States at the height of the hot war with Iran was inflicted by inexpensive drone swarms launched by the theocratic state.

insights.claim: Eric Schmidt warns that as AI gets better at finding hidden submarines and mobile launchers, the second-strike capability premise underpinning 70 years of nuclear stability disintegrates.
insights.speaker: NATHAN GARDELS
insights.so_what: AI counterforce capability is a tail-risk geopolitical destabilizer worth monitoring alongside arms-control diplomacy.
insights.quote: As AI gets better at finding the hidden submarines or mobile launchers, however, that premise disintegrates.

insights.claim: Eric Schmidt notes data centers and computer centers that train AI could become military targets in their own right, and that the US, China and Russia should be negotiating limits as Cold War powers did on testing and delivery systems.
insights.speaker: NATHAN GARDELS
insights.so_what: Implies AI compute infrastructure carries latent strategic-target risk; watch for AI arms-control talks among the three powers.
insights.quote: the data centers and computer centers that train AI might look like targets in their own right.

insights.claim: The article states frontier AI models such as Anthropic's Mythos can spot and exploit software vulnerabilities humans cannot, opening the door to cyberattacks on critical infrastructure and potentially nuclear targeting systems.
insights.speaker: NATHAN GARDELS
insights.so_what: Frontier-model cyber-offense capability raises critical-infrastructure cyber-risk and demand for AI-driven defense.
insights.quote: We've now seen how powerful frontier AI models like Anthropic's Mythos can spot and exploit software vulnerabilities that humans can't, opening the door to cyberattacks on critical infrastructure by bad actors.
insights.locator: We've now seen how powerful frontier AI models
